pr.title: ft: improve Crazy Hand init command dispatch
pr.author: itsgrimetime

summary: Draft PR improving the unmatched Crazy Hand init dispatcher `ftCh_Init_80156AD8`. It corrected the human command button mapping, especially the A/Z dispatch swap, changed pad-button checks to a style closer to the matched Master Hand dispatcher, and replaced raw `0`/`2` float literals with named data symbols. It improved the decomp.dev/checkdiff score from about 98.86% to 98.99% but remained non-matching due to pad-word register allocation drift. The PR was closed unmerged after a collaborator commented that the work was already done.
what_changed: Removed the local `static inline u32 btn_pressed(u32 mask)` helper and replaced it with a scoped `#define buttons (HSD_PadMasterStatus[3].button)` around `ftCh_Init_80156AD8`.
Cached `u32 l_pressed = buttons & HSD_PAD_L` and reused it for the L+DPad branches, matching the style described in the PR body as similar to a matched Master Hand dispatcher.
Rewrote the remaining command checks to direct `(buttons & HSD_PAD_*)` expressions for R, B, A, Z, and Y combinations.

Fixed the human command dispatch mapping by swapping the A/Z paths: Z+DPadUp/Right now selects `ftMh_MS_BackAirPlane2`/`ftMh_MS_BackAirplane3` via `ftCh_Init_80158B3C`, while A+DPadUp/Right/Down now calls `ftCh_Init_801597F0` or `ftCh_Init_80159F40`.
Replaced random-position `* 2` multipliers with `* ftCh_Init_804DA060` in the `HSD_Randf` position range math.
Added `extern f32 ftCh_Init_804DA05C;` and `extern f32 ftCh_Init_804DA060;` declarations to `ftCh_Init.h`.
smart_moves: Used the known-matching sibling implementation as a style guide: caching only the L-button test is a targeted register/codegen tactic rather than a broad rewrite.
Kept the temporary `buttons` macro scoped with `#undef buttons`, limiting macro pollution while still forcing direct pad-word expressions instead of an inline helper.
Improved both correctness and matching at once by fixing the button mapping instead of treating dispatch order as only a cosmetic decomp issue.
Named float constants at their data addresses so Vec3 zeroing and random range math can use the same symbol references as the target instead of raw literals.
Documented exact verification and remaining drift, including `checkdiff` output and the known `r0` target versus `r5` current pad-word register allocation mismatch.
decomp_lessons: For dispatcher-style fighter code, compare against matched sibling dispatchers such as Master Hand before inventing helper abstractions; expression shape can affect register allocation.
Inline helpers that look cleaner in C may hurt matching when the target repeatedly reads or masks a global pad word; direct macros or expressions can better preserve target codegen.
When the original uses float data symbols for `0.0f` or `2.0f`, replacing numeric literals with named `extern f32` constants can reduce drift in Vec3 setup and arithmetic.
Button-map fixes should be checked as functional behavior, not just matching noise; this PR changed which commands are selected by A versus Z combinations.
A partial match improvement is still valuable if the remaining non-match is isolated and documented, especially when the unresolved issue is specific register allocation.
naming_conventions: `ftCh_` names are used for Crazy Hand functions, including address-based unnamed symbols such as `ftCh_Init_80156AD8`.
Unidentified data constants in this module were exposed as address-based `f32` symbols: `ftCh_Init_804DA05C` and `ftCh_Init_804DA060`.
The header change used `extern f32` declarations for module-level constants rather than local magic literals.
Existing motion-state names such as `ftMh_MS_BackAirplane2`, `ftMh_MS_BackAirplane3`, `ftMh_MS_Squeezing1`, `ftMh_MS_Squeeze`, `ftMh_MS_Throw`, and `ftMh_MS_Slam` were preserved.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Replace a small inline helper with direct global pad-word access when the target appears sensitive to load/mask expression shape.
Cache `buttons & HSD_PAD_L` in a local `l_pressed` variable while leaving other button checks as direct masks to mimic the matched dispatcher pattern.
Use address-named float constants for `0.0f` and `2.0f` to encourage symbol loads and avoid literal-folding differences.
Run targeted function checkdiff: `python tools/checkdiff.py ftCh_Init_80156AD8 --no-tty --format json` reported 98.99317%.
The remaining known mismatch was not solved: full pad-word register allocation still targeted `r0` while the current code used `r5`, with derived mask-temp register differences.
review_feedback: decomp.dev reported one unmatched-item improvement for `main/melee/ft/chara/ftCrazyHand/ftCh_Init`: `ftCh_Init_80156AD8` moved from 98.86% to 98.99%, shown as `+2` bytes.
Collaborator jellejurre commented: "Already done by me".
There were no line-level review comments or formal reviews in the provided slice.
follow_up_queries: Find the PR or commit by jellejurre that already implemented the Crazy Hand init command dispatcher work.
Inspect the matched Master Hand dispatcher and compare its pad-button caching/register allocation pattern to current `ftCh_Init_80156AD8`.
Search current master for `ftCh_Init_804DA05C` and `ftCh_Init_804DA060` to verify whether these constants are now declared and used.
Check whether the A/Z Crazy Hand human command mapping is correct in the final merged code.
Investigate the remaining pad-word register allocation drift where the target uses `r0` but the current code uses `r5`.
